sentences.text: From Theresienstadt.
That's right.
About three months before us came another transport of 5,000 people.
And they built up this so-called Czech Familienlager, the Czech Family Camp.
And they had a very similar treatment to us.
They came also with children, old people, and everyone in the camp.
And the leader from the Jewish side of the camp was Fredy Hirsch, who built a special block for youth-- for children-- the block 31.
And there were two blocks for children-- for very small children with mothers, [NON-ENGLISH] just--
Babies-- which was block 29, and the block 31 for children about-- from very small-- let's say from 6 till 14, or something like that.
Now, I come back to the sauna, where we got these strange clothes.
After a day, we came back, and we were tattooed.
We got a number.
In Auschwitz, you were happy if you got a number, because--
Yeah, of course.
The people who didn't got a number were usually sent-- off the shore to bury.
You can see my number is a little-- not straight.
Why?
Because two people from the politic department who came to tattooed us, took my hand, and it was very painful.
It was 132-- [LAUGHS] --dots.
I counted them during the time.
And since then, I ceased to be Yehudah Bakon.
I was only number 168, 194.
That was my only name.
It was very important you should remember it very well, because if-- there were many tragic circumstances that somebody spelled it wrongly, and it cost his life.
Anyhow, we got a number.
And two or three days later came the other half of the transport-- they had the same treatment.
During the time we were divided into man sections and woman sections.
After a while, I could see my mother and my sister, of course, and my father.
And slowly, after a short time, Fredy Hirsch came and took the youngsters to the children block.
The children block was outstanding in the way that there was no children in Auschwitz, except in a neighbor camp.
For a very short time there were children in the Zigeunerlager, in the gypsy camp.
But they were sent to the gas chambers in end of July, the beginning of August '44.
But a children compound like we had was completely--
Out of-- in whole Auschwitz.
Now, were not sent to outside work.
There were some people who had all kinds of odd jobs.
They were just keeping the mothers, and children, and elderly people.
And only a few commanders liked people who worked in the kitchen, or people.
I remember, the-- who worked with a wagon, who collected the so-called normal dying people, which were a tremendous lot, especially in the first days after our arrival.
I remember heaps of dead, violet-looking--
--bodies, some of which where choked on the way when they arrived, or who died shortly afterwards.
And these old people suffered the most of them-- mostly.
And though, in the beginning, there were really 50 or hundreds daily who died.
And slowly, it went down.
There was some people who committed suicide, and we saw it every morning how they--
--took them from the electric fence.
Now, the organization of our daily life was much better because of Fredy Hirsch.
Fredy Hirsch was the youth leader even before he came from Germany-- is what I know.
He was the head of the youth organization in Theresienstadt.

And in Auschwitz, he succeeded to put this idea into the Germans' head that it will be much better if the children will not disturb the life in the camps, because children are going back and forwards-- to put them in a special block.
And slowly, he managed to got many better condition.
One of them-- the formidable thing is that we could have our so-called appel-- counting-- inside the block.
The normal way was twice a day counting, which was a terrible torture.
We were put out in-- in all conditions of-- this terrible winter in-- in Auschwitz, in Birkenau.
Or all the terrible summers drained us, no matter what were-- people were falling down and dying-- and endless counting.
Because some of the SS men were very primitive and couldn't get their numbers straight.
Anyhow, he arranged for us to be counted inside the block, which saved us from many deaths.
Outside.
Secondly, he got better treatment for us, better food, special rations.
Yeah.
They could have--
No.
There were only about 3,000-- a half or so, because many died from illnesses, and so on.
They were there, yeah, yeah.
There were-- as us-- everything--
--children, babies, everything.
And there were even one or two blocks, [GERMAN].
I think it was number 28 and--
Opposite
Yes.
Why?
I can tell you on their perspective, which I know from now.
The German needed the Czech camp, of family camp, as a showcase for the possibility of the International Red Cross Committee.
And the International Red Cross Committee wanted to know what happened to the Jews.
So they had the one bluff, Theresienstadt.
They needed to say that even people who are leaving Theresienstadt are in a special camp, where they have children, and everything.
That was the main reason to have some alibi before the International Red Cross.
And we know that later on, in '44, they came-- International Red Cross Commission-- to Theresienstadt.
It was a tremendous show.
And that the show should work well, they sent--
--thousands of people to the east.
And they made it kind of a--
No, no.
I wasn't there.
But I heard it from the people who arrived.
A recreation home--
--and children playing, and the Germans--
--made a film--
--that Hitler give a-- a town to the Jews, where they have everything, and so on.
But to make it possible-- in Theresienstadt it was a bang.
There was-- all-- court-- if people were caught in--
--if somebody said thief, he was jailed.
But people didn't know in the court that after the jail, he was sent to the east.
That actually, if somebody stole some potatoes-- and he got the prison of two weeks-- it was actually meaning that afterwards, he goes to the east-- I mean, to his death.
But there was this kind of course.
There was culture life, a tremendous culture life.
Yeah, there was a bank with money.
You couldn't buy hardly anything.
There was some [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you say [NON-ENGLISH]?
Mustard.
You could buy a tiny little box of mustard.
But it was worthless.
But if you worked, you get a kind of book.
All was a great, great show, mainly--
--for covering-- deceit for the International German Cross.
And because of the International German Cross was so taken by the bluff-- and today we know there were-- some of them were great-- Swiss Nationalist.
They didn't like so much the Jews.
And they wrote a beautiful report.
The German had this report.
The foreign officers said, now we don't need anymore the coverage of the Czech transport.
And they went to the gas.
Anyhow, even if on arrival, we had to play the role only for six months.
In our Kartotek, in our own files, it was written-- six months, SB, which meant Sonderbehandlung in that language.
After six months, they should go--
Special treatment, meaning to go to gas.
But six months, we have to--
--to play this life.
And therefore, the first transport who came with us were--
--were sent to gas exactly after six months, on the 7th of March '44.
But there was a whole story.
How it happened, I can't give you details-- it's Fredy Hirsch and so on.
Yeah.
He was one of them.
He had a chance to--
--save his life-- like Korczak-- very similar.
But he went-- he killed-- I mean, he poisoned himself.
I met him a day before, because I knew him on the children block.
He took some pills which he had.
And they prepared some uprising, but it didn't came to it.
Only some doctors, twins, and some people who were sick at that time-- for one reason or another-- and were on the block of the sick people, survived.
Because the devious thing of the Germans, they knew sick people, of course they to gas.

And here you see, they leave sick people in the camp, which means you other people, surely you go to other working place-- and also distributed between the SS, to us, that we go to Heydebreck and all kinds of places-- I mean, this other transport.
But we knew that soon will be an appel-- I mean, call-- and all people from the older transport had to go a different way.
And so it was.
We knew them, and they took them to another camp in the so-called A compound.
We were in the B compound.
The camps were A, B, C, D, E, F, G.
And the A compound was a transitory camp.
B was the Czech family camp where I-- most of the time, I was there.
C were Hungarian women.
D was the Mannerlager, where I later came, and I will tell you about it.
The E was the gypsy camp, where I also worked, and I will tell you about it.
And then were-- F was-- FG were some barracks for the clothes , for the tremendous clothes, which they took from all the transport.
And then was the crematorium and sauna.
Now to coming back to-- to our life, it was a completely irrational life.
The SS came to visit us.
We were-- ours is of a-- half centi for Auschwitz circumstances.
Because we had still hair on the head.
We had civil clothes-- I mean-- [LAUGHS] not the nicest, but--
But not the stripes.
Some had it.
But most of them-- we could perform in the children block.
I know exactly what we performed.
We were divided in little groups.
We-- we recited all kinds of Czech songs.
Or we even had Oneg Shabbat, some of Madrichim.
I remember one Oneg Shabbat where the madrich told us, why?
We're children.
What would happen if there wouldn't be the magnetism of our Earth?
We could jump and fly to the moon.
Another madricha-- who is still alive, Ruth Bondi-- read us from a book about the life of little animals, by the famous writer, Karel Capek.
We had a tiny library.
It was in a wooden box from some--
Yeah.
Auschwitz-- Birkenau, because we're anyhow-- we had to die.
So the Germans said, we don't care what you do.
Do it in the best way which you and us should profit.
We knew.
And we knew-- we knew we have in our Kartotek six months.
And we knew later on, of course, when the whole transport went to gas.
And we knew afterwards who, and what, and how it happens.
But I want to tell you a little about this-- famous children camp, number 31.
We sang songs-- let's say one tune-- was the whole years I will-- and I tried to ask people who know music from varied camps.
Because I was--
'Dona Nobis Pacem'.
And only-- [LAUGHS] about a year ago, somebody who is a singer and knows very, very old German songs said, well, that's a old Lutheran--
[SINGING 'DONA NOBIS PACEM'] Dona, nobis, pacem.
Dona, nobis, pacem.
And we-- were singing from the 9th Symphony, as I mentioned a previous time.
We performed it for the Germans sometimes.
And if the German didn't like it-- especially our-- the head of our camp was a German Reich Deutsche with a green triangle-- Arno Bohm.
He had number 8-- he was a kill-- a real-- criminal.
If he didn't like something, he took his whistle and-- [LAUGHS] stop.
Later on, Willy Brachmann, who was his kapo of the camp, took over.
And the other kapo was Ada Fischer, who was the hangman from Theresienstadt.
I told you about the hanging.
He was a very strange man.
He said, I will do it, because for me, it's easy.
And he liked the children.
To us--
He was, I think, Czech-- Czech from Prague or somewhere.
And in our camp, he became a kapo of the camp later.
And to us children, he shouted and screamed, and went with his stick.
But he never beat up children.
No, no, no.
He was one of us.
But the-- the SS, the leader of our family camp was Bundrok.
He was a murderer.
He could take a stick and put it on a-- on a--
--throat of somebody, and, you know, and-- until he killed him.
And he could beat up people in the most brutal way till he killed him.
Yeah, yeah.
There was a lot of brutal--
Yeah, it happened.
But altogether, it was nothing to compare.

But we saw and heard, and what we later have been in other camps-- let's say we could see the camp number one, the quarantine camp, where we saw the very first time Muselmann, where it was-- Muselmann in Auschwitz was somebody who lost his will to live.
It has nothing to do with the Arabic.
Of course.
He was--
--bones and skin--
--and a glary look.
And we knew he can't last for another week or so.
He didn't care anything for-- and sometimes we found there-- somebody was a Czech.
And I remember his name was a Dubek.
And we spoke with him.
And we even gave him a piece of bread, because we didn't have much.
But he was in such a terrible shape.
We throw him over some bread.
Bench.
A piece-- one piece of two people-- this hanging-- with hanging hands of the dead corpse.
That was the daily thing we saw.
Yes, in this children block, when we sang Dona Nobis--
It's difficult to say, but about 150.
We were divided in little groups of little children and elderly children, and Fredy Hirsch.
This is--
They would be about six.
And they were singing and doing--
Comparatively they were happy.
But in the minute they brought in the food, nobody could anymore concentrate.
But let's say when we sang this song of 'Dona Nobis Pacem', I remember the SS men asked one of our-- Fredy Hirsch, he asked, what-- why are you asking for peace?
Why are you singing?
He just said.
And Fredy was very clever and very German.
And he-- he spoke the same language like them.
And he always was looking very clean and proper.
He even dared to speak to German, which was outstanding.
He said, because we have peace, therefore, we can sing.
But the Germans said, we people have to be on the front and--
--fight.
Do you have peace?
And he said because--
Well, because we have a peace, [NON-ENGLISH], I think he answered him.
And nothing happened.
I remember, let's say, some performance where we performed, we children, a kind of cabaret.
And we said, what shall happen after the war?
And then we said, well, we see these big trucks, the military trucks, and everyone is running away.
Because we are afraid it goes to the gas chambers.
Yeah.
But on the other hand--
Yeah.
We had all kinds of performance.
My job was to prepare children's soup and to heat the oven.
In-- in these barracks, there were oven from both sides.
And we were two boys.
And we got much more-- coal?
How do I say it?
It?
Coal to heat.
And all-- but people-- there was a place for coal.
But nobody wanted to take it, because it was--
--so difficult to-- it was--
--muck.
No, no no.
To go there--
--it was very difficult to go.
Nobody wanted to go in this mud and get dirty.
But we have-- we had competition between our children.
Who will bring most of the--
--coal.
And whoever group brought most of the coal got a little more soup, or something like that.
Fredy achieved also that we got from the packages, food packages, which arrived to Jews or Polish prisoners which had died, meanwhile.
So what happened to it?
There was a post office in each of the big blocks.
And he got the packages.
Of course, the SS took out the best things, but we got some rotten-- or half rotten things.
And we throw it in the soup.
And I remember once, I found a little glass tube in the bread, which had some German marks.
And I remember I could buy with it a bit of bread and help my parents.
Diarrhea.
And there was a--
--dysentery, all kinds of things.
Because they got the black bread, we got the white bread.
But if you could roast it, this bread, you could eat something.
And there was a kind of business going on for, let's say, two pieces of black bread.
You gave us one bread.
On the ovens, we had the possibility to roast it to them.
That was one of the jobs.
The other jobs was we were also gardening--
--the gate.
Because Fredy Hirsch had a little-- we called it kumba-- a little tiny place.
Where always the block [NON-ENGLISH] had a tiny little room for himself.
And Fredy had it.
And all-- every block had this in each of the blocks.
And that we knew later, there was one SS man-- Pestek was his name.
He--
Pestek.
He fell in love with one Jewish-- a woman-- Renee Neumann.
And he wanted to take her out.
And he even succeeded to take out one prisoner, Lederer.
He smuggled him a-- a German uniform, and he took him out.
And this Lederer came later to Theresienstadt and told the people what's going on.
Auschwitz, yes.
It was one-- he came later on, back again.
He wanted to take this-- his-- this woman.
No, he was denounced.
And-- in the German court.
And he was killed.
Yeah, he was a German SS.
But was a good one.
No. the woman-- as far as I know-- survived.
Anyhow, this Pestek had always all kinds of talks with Fredy Hirsch.
And if he came and had to talk to Fredy Hirsch, I had to be outside and have a good look-- that if Bundrok or other Germans come, I had to--
--warn them.
Because sometimes Mengele come.
Opposite was the-- krankenblock.
And Mengele came often to visit them.
He came also to us.
Midgets.
He-- he wanted gypsies and midgets.
Maybe there was one, but whoever was there, when Mengele took him, there was, I remember, two-- I mean two couples.
I remember one very well-- [NON-ENGLISH] brother.
But Mengele took them to his compound in the Zigeunerlager, where all the--
--they were kept there.
And later on I will tell you we visited this block.
And I can tell you more about it.
But now, with this kinderblock-- so we were guarding Pestek and-- with Fredy Hirsch, who had all kinds of talks.
And there were rumors they are preparing here for a kind of uprising--
--which didn't came through.
Yeah, which didn't happen.
And what happened, as I told you, on about the 6th of March, this--
--the-- '44, this one group of people who came before us went to the other camp.
And only some sick people and-- were left, and doctors and nurses.
Shall I?
Yeah, a break.
